question: Define graph colouring and chromatic number. What is the chromatic number of K₄ and C₅?
expectedPoints: Graph colouring assigns colours to vertices such that adjacent vertices have different colours
Chromatic number χ(G) is minimum colours needed for proper colouring
χ(K₄) = 4 (complete graph on 4 vertices)
χ(C₅) = 3 (odd cycle requires 3 colours)

question: State and explain the Four Color Theorem. Why was its proof significant?
expectedPoints: Four Color Theorem: Every planar graph can be coloured with at most 4 colours
Proof by Appel and Haken in 1976
First major theorem proved using computer assistance
Significance: Computational proof revolutionized mathematics

question: What is greedy colouring algorithm? Explain with an example.
expectedPoints: Greedy colouring: order vertices and assign smallest available colour
Depends on vertex ordering - different orders give different results
Example: For path v1-v2-v3-v4, ordering v1,v2,v3,v4 gives 2 colours
Worst case uses Δ(G)+1 colours

question: Prove that every bipartite graph has chromatic number 2.
expectedPoints: Bipartite graph can be partitioned into two independent sets
All edges go between the two partitions
Vertices in same partition are not adjacent - can share colour
Assign colour 1 to one partition, colour 2 to other → proper 2-colouring

question: State Brooks' Theorem and identify the exceptions.
expectedPoints: Brooks' Theorem: For connected G not Kₙ or odd cycle, χ(G) ≤ Δ(G)
Exceptions: Complete graphs Kₙ (χ = Δ+1 = n-1+1 = n)
Odd cycles (C₂ₖ₊₁): χ = 3 = Δ = 2 + 1
These achieve Δ(G)+1, others are bounded by Δ(G)

question: Define chromatic polynomial. What is P(K₂, k)?
expectedPoints: Chromatic polynomial P(G,k) counts number of proper k-colourings
It is a polynomial in k of degree |V|
For K₂ (2 vertices, 1 edge): colour first vertex k ways, second vertex (k-1) ways
P(K₂,k) = k(k-1) = k² - k

question: What is the relationship between clique number ω(G) and chromatic number χ(G)? Give an example.
expectedPoints: Always true: ω(G) ≤ χ(G) for any graph
Because vertices in a clique are all pairwise adjacent, need different colours
For K₅: ω = 5, χ = 5 (equality)
For odd cycle C₅: ω = 2, χ = 3 (strict inequality)

question: Define proper colouring and chromatic number. Prove that χ(G) ≥ ω(G) for any graph G. Also find χ and ω for graph shown: K₃,₃.
expectedPoints: Proper colouring: adjacent vertices get different colours
Chromatic number χ(G): minimum colours needed for proper colouring
ω(G): size of largest clique
Proof: vertices in a clique are all adjacent, need different colours → need at least ω colours
K₃,₃ is bipartite → χ = 2; no triangle → ω = 2
Thus χ(K₃,₃) = ω(K₃,₃) = 2

question: Apply greedy colouring algorithm to the given graph with vertices in order v1,v2,v3,v4,v5,v6. Determine the colours assigned and number of colours used. Graph: path v1-v2-v3, and separate path v4-v5-v6, plus edge v3-v4.
expectedPoints: Greedy algorithm: process vertices in given order
v1: colour 1 (no neighbours coloured)
v2: neighbour v1 has colour 1 → colour 2
v3: neighbours v1,v2 have colours 1,2 → colour 3 (or 1 if available)
v4: neighbour v3 has colour 3 → colour 1 (available)
v5: neighbours v4 has colour 1 → colour 2
v6: neighbour v5 has colour 2 → colour 3 or 1
Depending on choices, uses 3 colours (worst case) or 2 (optimal)
Maximum degree Δ = 2, greedy uses ≤ 3 colours

question: State and prove the Five Color Theorem for planar graphs.
expectedPoints: Five Color Theorem: Every planar graph can be coloured with at most 5 colours
Proof by induction on number of vertices
Base case: n ≤ 5 trivial
Inductive step: For n ≥ 6, planar graph has vertex v with degree ≤ 5 (by handshaking lemma)
Remove v, colour remaining n-1 vertices by induction (uses ≤ 5 colours)
If deg(v) ≤ 4, v has unused colour among 5 → colour v
If deg(v) = 5, neighbours have at most 5 colours; use Kempe chain argument to free a colour
Thus planar graph is 5-colourable

question: Find the chromatic number of the following graphs: (i) K₆ (ii) C₈ (iii) W₇ (wheel with 7 vertices) (iv) Complete bipartite graph K₄,₅
expectedPoints: (i) K₆: χ = 6 (complete graph on 6 vertices)
(ii) C₈: χ = 2 (even cycle is bipartite)
(iii) W₇: Wheel with odd number (7) → χ = 4 (3-cycle hub plus odd outer cycle needs 4th colour)
(iv) K₄,₅: χ = 2 (bipartite, all bipartite graphs have χ = 2)
Explanation: Kₙ needs n colours, Cₙ has χ=2 for even n, Wₙ has χ=4 for odd n≥7, bipartite always 2
